This letter is in response to your letter or ... JUne 28, 1979 
and subsequent telephone conversations regarding the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
I hope 
the following advice provided pursuant to Government Code Section 
83114(b) is helpful. 
The facts as you have provided them and 
as I understand them are set forth below. 
You are a member of the Sacramento County Board of Super-
visors and the sole owner of the rN"ade Advertising Agency. 
The 
Agency represents a number of clients some of whom request ad-
ve:::tising space on billboards kno'\m as "bulletins". 
It is 
customary that when an advertising agency contracts for bulletin 
space, it receives a commission of between 15 and 17% from the 
bulletin company. 
During the past twelve months, your firm 
has received more than $250 in commissions frem Foster and 
Kleiser, a nation-wide bulletin company. 
You have asked whether you should disqualify yourselz 
f:::om making or participating in the making of decisions re-
garding the constr~ction or removal of a bulletin ow~ed by 
Foster ~nd Kleiser in Carmichael.

87100,

The term "financial interest" is defir:.ed in Section 87103 
to mean, 
An 
of~icial has a financial interes~ in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect. distin-
guishable from its effect on the ?ublic generally,

Section 82030 Drovides that income of an individual in-
cludes income received by a business entity in which the official 
has a 107, or greater interest. 
Since vou are the sale mvner of 
your advertising firm, Foster and Kleiser is a source of income 
to you by virtue of the co~missions it pays to your advertising 
agency, and as it is clearly foreseeable that decisions con-
cerning the bulletin will affect Foster and Kleiser, the only 
remaining question is whether the effects of the decision will 
be material. 
Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a) 
provides that the effects of a decision are material if they 
will have a significant effect on a source of income. 
2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1) and (3) of the regulation states 
that the effect of a decision on a business entity which is a

BOARD OF SUPERVISORS 
COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO 
700 Ii STRe:ET 
/ 
SACRAMENTO. CA. 95814 
June 28, 1979 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Attention: 
Ted 
~i~ 
Conflict of Interest Division 
Request No_. _____ _ 
,/ :::? '79 
Date Received 
.7"" - '-'-

Response Due ,- - I T-
/-' / 
PROPOSED RESPON§E 
DUE: '7 - / ;; - 71: 
) 
FRED G. WADE 
SUPE:RVISOR FOURTH OISTRICT 
Te:L.EPHONe: (9161 440-5491 
Re: 
Request for Opinion Pertaining to Conflict of Interest 
Gentlemen: 
I represent the Fourth Supervisorial District as a member of the Board 
of Supervisors of Sacramento County. 
I have been a member of the Board 
of Supervisors since 1975. 
Certain members of the community have raised the question as to whether 
I possessed a financial interest, and should have disqualified myself 
from voting upon, a zoning matter which was recently acted upon by the 
Board of Supervisors. 
In my opinion, I did not possess a financial 
interest which would have disqualified me from voting. 
However, it is 
important for me to know for purposes of future actions whether the 
circumstances surrounding the transaction upon which I voted required 
me to disqualify myself in the opinion of the FPPC. 
If it should be 
determined that those circumstances constitute a disqualification, I 
will be better able to identify circumstances which require disqualifi-
cation in the future. 
For the foregoing reasons I am requesting an opinion by the Fair Politi-
cal Practices Comnission as to whether I possessed a financial interest 
which required me to disqualify myself from voting upon the transaction 
described below. 
Toward the end of 1978, the firm of Foster and Kleiser applied to the 
County of Sacramento for a conditional use permit under the County1s 
Zoning Ordinance to construct a billboard on a particular parcel of pro-
perty. 
On February 26, 1979, the application was considered by the 
Project P1anning Commission during a public hearing. 
The Planning Com-
mission denied the application. 
Foster and Kleiser appealed the denial 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
Attention: 
Ted Prim 
June 28, 1979 
Page 2 
(as is permitted by the Zoning Ordinance) to the Board of Supervisors. 
On April 12, 1979, the Board of Supervisors considered the appeal during 
a public hearing. 
At the close of the hearing the Board reversed the 
decision of the Project Planning Commission, and granted the use permit 
for the billboard, subject to various conditions. 
The vote by which the 
permit was issued was three in favor and two against. 
My vote was one 
of the three which was in favor. 
Foster and Kleiser is a national outdoor advertising company, which does 
business in Sacramento County. 
A segment of its business operations in-
volves the acquisition of sites adjacent to roadways, and the construction 
thereon of billboards. 
The company sells space on the billboards to 
private and public enterprises for advertising purposes. 
The sign mes-
sages are presented in behalf of the enterprises which purchase the space. 
and Foster and Kleiser implements the message on the billboard by paint-
ing or posting sc~eened paper. 
Foster and Kleiser promulgates a standardized series of rates for the 
purchase of billboard space. 
These rates are published, and applied 
uniformly by Foster and Kleiser within Sacramento County to all prospec-
tive purchasers of space. 
There are at least two other outdoor advertising companies which do 
business in Sacramento County. 
These are National and Eller. 
Their 
marketing practices are substantially similar to those of Foster and 
Kleiser, described above. 
I am the owner of an advertising agency, which is located in and does 
business in Sacramento County. 
The agency is incorporated, and I am the 
sole stockholder. 
My agency provides advice to its clientele concerning 
promotional and advertising matters, develops advertising strategy, 
creates copy and designs layout, and arranges for the placement of the 
advertising with media. 
A small portion of the agency's media placement 
consists of arrangement for billboard space. 
When arranging for outdoor advertising space, the agency contacts firms 
such as Foster and Kleiser, submits the layout, identifies the size and 
number of billboards which the agency's client desires to purchase, and 
otherwise makes arrangements for contracts, billings, etc. 
A contract 
is entered into between the agency's client and the outdoor advertising 
company. 
My agency signs the contract in the name of the client. 
My agency's income is derived from two principal sources. 
The first is 
fees from clients which are charged primarily on a time and material 
basis, for such services as creating copy and designing layout. 
The 
second is commissions as a result of placement of advertising with such 
media as radio and television stations. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
Attention: 
Ted Prim 
June 28, 1979 
Page 3 
The standard rates for billboard space charged by Foster and Kleiser 
are charged uniformly to all persons, businesses and entities which 
place advertising with that company. 
However, if advertising is placed 
by an advertising agency in behalf of a client, a 16-2/3% discount from 
the standard rate is granted by Foster and Kleiser. 
The 16-2/3% dis-
count is retained by the advertising agency as a commission. 
Thus, the 
client pays to the advertising agency the standard fee imposed by Foster 
and Kleiser, the advertising agency retains 16-2/3% of that amount, and 
remits the balance to Foster and Kleiser as payment for the billboard 
space. 
The contract between the client and Foster and Kleiser provides 
for the discount rate and commission retention by the advertising agency. 
r understand that if an advertising agency is not recognized or does not 
have an established credit rating, Foster and Kleiser requires that the 
fee be paid directly by the client, and then remits the 16-2/3% discount 
to the advertising agency. 
The contract makes the client, and not the 
advertising agency, liable for the fee. 
To my knowledge the 16-2/3% discount rate is standard throughout the 
billboard industry, with one exception. 
National Advertising Company 
has established a discount rate of only 15%. 
To the best of my knowledge neither Foster and Kleiser nor any other 
billboard company varies its standard fees or percentage of discount 
based upon volume of business which a client or advertising agency does 
with the billboard company. 
Nor, to my knowledge, does Foster and 
Kleiser or any other billboard company grant any preference in location 
of placement of billboard advertising based upon volume of business 
which a client or advertising agency does with that company. 
On April 12, 1979, when I voted on the zoning matter described above, 
my agency had three clients with existing contracts with Foster and 
Kleiser for outdoor advertising space. 
One of those clients was Capital 
Federal Savings and Loan. 
The contract which Capital Federal Savings 
and Loan had with Foster and Kleiser expired June 2, 1979, and was not 
renewed. 
Construction of the billboard authorized by the approval on 
April 12, 1979, was completed sometime around the end of Mayor beginning 
of June, 1979. 
Sometime after the billboard was completed, Foster and 
Kleiser, without my request, consent or knowledge, and so far as r know, 
without that of Capital Federal Savings and Loan, installed the adver-
tising display for Capital Federal Savings and Loan (which had recently 
been removed from another billboard as a result of expiration of the 
contract) on the new billboard which had been approved. 
The date of 
installation is unknown. 
r saw the display on June 13, 1979. 
The dis-
play was replaced shortly thereafter. 
I presume, though I do not know, that Foster and Kleiser made the in-
stallation of the Capital Federal Savings and Loan advertising for the 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
Attention: 
Ted Prim 
June 28, 1979 
Page 4 
purpose of covering up blank space on the new billboard until the 
billboard could be covered with sold space. 
During the period April, 1978, through April, 1979, my agency received 
more than $250 in commissions from clients as a result of advertising 
placed with Foster and Kleiser. 
My proprietary interest in the adver-
tising agency would be valued at more than $1,000. 
I have no investment 
in Foster and Kleiser and no relationship with that firm, except as 
described above. 
Briefly stated, my belief that I did not possess a financial interest 
which disqualified me from voting is based on the fact that the source 
of the commission is the fee paid by my agency's client to Foster and 
Kleiser. 
My agency purchases, in behalf of named clients, billboard 
space from Foster and Kleiser. 
The fees for such space are standard-
ized, as is the aiscount which becomes my agency's commission retained 
from the fees paid by the client. 
I have no interest in Foster and 
Kleiser, financial or otherwise. 
The gratuitous placement by Foster 
and Kleiser of my client's advertising on the newly constructed bill-
board was, to the best of my knowledge, a coincidental product of 
Foster and Kleiser's desire to cover a newly constructed billboard and 
the availability of a recently removed display as a result of expira-
tion of that client's contract with Foster and Kleiser. 
Please do not hesitate to call me if you need additional information or 
wish to discuss the facts relating to this request.

sections.question: Whether you should disqualify yourself from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding the construction or removal of a bulletin owned by Foster and Kleiser in Carmichael, given that your advertising agency receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser for arranging billboard advertising space.
sections.conclusion: On the basis of this information, we conclude that you are not disqualified from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's Carmichael bulletin.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a County Supervisor with a sole-owned advertising agency that receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser have a disqualifying financial interest in decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's billboard construction permit?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Supervisor is not disqualified from participating in decisions regarding the Foster and Kleiser bulletin because the financial effect would not be material under applicable regulations, as the bulletin's annualized gross revenues fall below materiality thresholds and the Supervisor would receive no direct benefit from the specific decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Complete advice letter with question from incoming request and FPPC conclusion. Contains detailed legal analysis citing Government Code sections and administrative regulations. Minor OCR artifacts in document but key information clearly extracted. Includes incoming request letter (June 28, 1979) followed by FPPC response (September 27, 1979).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether you should disqualify yourself from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding the construction or removal of a bulletin owned by Foster and Kleiser in Carmichael, given that your advertising agency receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser for arranging billboard advertising space.

CONCLUSION: On the basis of this information, we conclude that you are not disqualified from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's Carmichael bulletin.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Sacramento County Supervisor Fred G. Wade that he is not disqualified from voting on a Foster and Kleiser billboard permit despite his sole ownership of an advertising agency that receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser, because the financial effects of the specific decision would not be material under Political Reform Act standards.
